message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 was responsible for an enormous global death toll with large variation among countries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To examine the possible impact of obesity on COVID-19 death rates.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Measure associations between obesity rates in 2016 and COVID-19 deaths/million population through 2/25/2021, across countries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Global</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>167 countries for which obesity and death data were available, grouped by population size, with multiples of 10 countries in each of 8 groups plus a group including all 57 countries with obesity rates <15%.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Outcome and measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Using Excel, COVID-19 deaths/million were regressed on the obesity rate for each country, based on obesity being a key factor in COVID hospitalizations and deaths. Using the least squares formula for the best fit for each model, R
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    , components of the formula, and the percentage of world population represented, were recorded for each group.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Obesity rates ranged from 2.1% to 37.9% and death rates ranged from 0.4/million to 1,892/million for groups representing up to 91% of global population. Results for the 8 population groups had R
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    from 0.30 to 0.90 with slopes of the fitted line ranging from 27.9-51.0. Countries with obesity rates <15% had consistently low death rates (≤233/million), R
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    of 0.003 and slope of the line=1.01.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>For most countries about one-third of the difference in COVID death rates was due to obesity while in countries with obesity <15%, consistently low death rates were not associated with obesity. Reduced obesity rates could potentially have lowered the COVID death toll.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Global association of obesity and COVID-19 death rates

reference.unstructured: The Epidemiological Characteristics of an Outbreak of 2019 Novel Coronavirus Diseases (COVID-19) - China CCDC, February 17 2020.
Report of the WHO-China Joint Mission on Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) [Pdf] - World Health Organization, Feb. 28, 2020

Adams ML , Katz DL , Grandpre J. Updated estimates of chronic conditions affecting risk for complications from coronavirus disease, United States. Emerg Infect Dis. 2020 Sep [date cited]. https://doi.org/10.3201/eid2609.20211
Worldometer. COVID-19 Coronavirus Pandemic. Updated February 25, 2021. https://www.worldometers.info/coronavirus/#countries. Accessed February 25, 2021.

Central Intelligence Agency. The World Factbook. Country comparison: obesity - adult prevalence rate 2016. https://web.archive.org/web/20200630202020/ https://www.cia.gov/library/publications/the-world-factbook/rankorder/2228rank.html. Accessed March 1, 2021.

reference.article-title: The impact of key modifiable risk factors on leading chronic conditions

reference.unstructured: Brownson RC , Remington PL , Wegner, MV . Chronic Disease Epidemiology and Control. 4th ed. Washington, DC: American Public Health Association; 2016.
